q: What remains constant no matter how a rigid body moves?[cite: 1]
a: The angular velocity of the body
The distance between any two particles within the body
The linear velocity of the body
The tangential acceleration of the particles

q: What defines a rigid body in the context of rotational motion?[cite: 1]
a: It does not deform or change shape
Its particles move in straight lines
It does not undergo circular motion

q: What type of motion do all constituting particles undergo if a rigid body is in rotational motion?[cite: 1]
a: Circular motion about a common axis

q: What is occurring when an object's angular velocity is changing?[cite: 1]
a: It has a constant angular acceleration
It is moving in a straight line

q: What are the kinematic equations for rotational motion derived in terms of?[cite: 1]
a: Angular displacement, angular velocity, and angular acceleration

q: Where does the centre of rotation lie for all particles of a rotating body?[cite: 1]
In what unit is angular displacement $\theta$ measured when using the equation $s = r \theta$?[cite: 1]
What is the relation between arc length $s$, radius $r$, and angular displacement $\theta$?[cite: 1]
What is true about the instantaneous angular velocity for all particles comprising a rotational object?[cite: 1]
a: It is the same for all particles
It varies depending on the radius
It is greatest at the edges
It is lowest at the axis

q: How is the tangential velocity defined in relation to the path of the particle?[cite: 1]
When an object's angular velocity changes, what other magnitude changes, resulting in tangential acceleration?[cite: 1]
How does the direction of the angular acceleration vector relate to the angular velocity when the angular velocity is decreasing?[cite: 1]
a: It points in the same direction
It points in the opposite to the angular velocity

q: What is true about the magnitude of velocity in uniform circular motion?[cite: 1]
Why is an object revolving in a circle continuously accelerating even if the speed is constant?[cite: 1]
a: Because the mass is constantly shifting
Because the direction of the velocity continuously changes

q: Where does the centripetal acceleration vector point in uniform circular motion?[cite: 1]
a: Towards the centre of the circular path

q: What is the mathematical relationship between centripetal acceleration $a_c$, linear speed $v$, and radius $r$?[cite: 1]
What is the angle between the centripetal acceleration vector and the linear velocity vector in uniform circular motion?[cite: 1]
What causes tangential acceleration in nonuniform circular motion?[cite: 1]
What is the resultant acceleration in nonuniform circular motion composed of?[cite: 1]
a: The difference between angular and linear accelerations
The vector sum of centripetal and tangential accelerations
